title: What Is Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?
content:
When a Phase I archaeological survey identifies a potentially significant site on your project area, federal regulations typically require a Phase II archaeological evaluation before construction can proceed. This critical step determines whether a site meets the criteria for listing on the National Register of Historic Places — a finding that can reshape project timelines, budgets, and designs.

Quick Answer: A Phase II archaeological evaluation is a targeted subsurface investigation that determines whether an identified archaeological site meets the National Register of Historic Places eligibility criteria. It involves controlled excavation, artifact analysis, and a professional assessment of the site's significance under 36 CFR Part 800 and Section 106 of the NHPA.

Why Is Phase II Archaeological Evaluation Required?

Phase II archaeological evaluation is required because federal law demands that project sponsors assess how undertakings affect historic properties before they proceed. Under Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act, any project receiving federal funding, permits, or licenses must evaluate whether identified archaeological sites qualify as historic properties.

A site identified during Phase I fieldwork may contain artifacts or features, but surface-level finds alone rarely provide enough data to judge significance. Phase II excavation goes deeper — literally — to answer the questions that determine regulatory outcomes. Without this step, agencies cannot make legally defensible decisions about project design or mitigation requirements.

For project sponsors such as state transportation departments, federal agencies, and private developers with federal nexus, skipping or inadequately performing a Phase II evaluation exposes the entire undertaking to legal challenge and costly delays downstream. A thorough evaluation conducted early in project development is almost always less expensive than resolving compliance failures after construction has begun.

What Does Phase II Archaeological Evaluation Involve?

Phase II archaeological evaluation involves systematic, controlled excavation designed to characterize a site's integrity, depth, content, and research potential. Archaeologists place hand-excavated test units — typically 1×1 meter or 1×2 meter squares — across the site to sample deposits in a statistically meaningful pattern.

Field crews screen all excavated soil through wire mesh to recover artifacts, features such as hearths or post molds, and ecofacts like faunal bone or botanical remains. Every provenience is mapped, photographed, and recorded to maintain the stratigraphic context that makes the data interpretable. Feature identification is especially important: intact features signal the kind of in-place archaeological context that supports National Register eligibility.

Laboratory analysis follows fieldwork. Artifacts are catalogued, identified, and dated; radiocarbon or other chronometric samples may be submitted; and specialists may assess faunal assemblages, ceramics, or lithic technology. The resulting data allow the principal investigator to evaluate whether the site retains integrity and possesses the significance required under National Register Criteria A through D.

How Is National Register Eligibility Determined?

National Register eligibility is determined by evaluating whether a site meets one or more of the four National Register criteria and retains sufficient integrity to convey its significance. Criteria A through D cover association with important historical events, significant persons, distinctive architectural character, and potential to yield important information through research.

For most archaeological sites evaluated in a Phase II archaeological evaluation, Criterion D — the capacity to yield important information — is the primary basis for eligibility. The Phase II report must demonstrate that the site contains well-preserved data sets capable of addressing meaningful research questions about prehistory, history, or both.

Integrity is equally important. A site that has been heavily disturbed by modern land use may retain interesting artifacts but lack the contextual integrity needed to support research conclusions. The evaluating archaeologist weighs integrity across seven aspects recognized by the National Park Service: location, design, setting, materials, workmanship, feeling, and association. The resulting eligibility recommendation guides the State Historic Preservation Office and the lead federal agency in making the formal determination.

What Happens After the Phase II Report Is Submitted?

After the Phase II archaeological evaluation report is submitted, the lead federal agency consults with the State Historic Preservation Office to formally determine whether the evaluated site is eligible for the National Register of Historic Places. This consultation follows the process outlined in 36 CFR Part 800.

If the site is found not eligible, and no other historic properties are affected, the Section 106 process concludes and the project may move forward without further archaeological constraint. If the site is determined eligible, the agency must assess the effect of the proposed undertaking on that historic property. A finding of adverse effect triggers Phase III data recovery or redesign to avoid or minimize the impact.

Project sponsors benefit from understanding that an eligibility determination is not automatically a project-stopper. Many eligible sites can be avoided through minor alignment shifts or design adjustments. Others are mitigated through excavation. Early coordination between the project team, the consulting archaeologist, and the SHPO usually produces the most cost-effective outcome. Historic preservation compliance planning integrated into early project design consistently reduces overall costs.

Who Can Conduct a Phase II Archaeological Evaluation?

Phase II archaeological evaluation must be conducted by archaeologists who meet the Secretary of the Interior's Professional Qualification Standards, which require a graduate degree in archaeology or a closely related field plus sufficient supervised field experience. Many states and federal agencies impose additional requirements beyond the federal baseline.

The lead archaeologist — typically the principal investigator — bears responsibility for research design, field oversight, laboratory direction, and the final eligibility recommendation. RPA-certified professionals, those holding the Register of Professional Archaeologists credential, have demonstrated compliance with a formal code of ethics and standards of research performance, making them a recognized benchmark for professional qualification.

Selecting a firm with regional expertise matters. Archaeological cultures, site types, artifact assemblages, and regulatory expectations vary across the Southeast. An archaeologist familiar with the specific region's prehistoric sequences and historic contexts brings interpretive depth that generic credentials alone cannot provide. Tennessee Valley Archaeological Research (TVAR) has conducted Phase I through Phase III investigations across Alabama and the broader Southeast for more than 14 years, with RPA-certified staff and in-house GIS and remote sensing capabilities that strengthen site characterization from the outset.

Frequently Asked Questions

How long does a Phase II archaeological evaluation take?

Timeline varies with site size and complexity, but most Phase II evaluations require two to six weeks of fieldwork followed by four to twelve weeks of laboratory analysis and report preparation. Large or complex sites, those with deeply buried deposits or extensive feature distributions, can take longer. Early coordination with your archaeologist and the reviewing agency helps set realistic schedules.

What is the difference between Phase I and Phase II archaeology?

Phase I is a reconnaissance-level survey designed to identify whether archaeological sites are present in a project area. Phase II is a targeted evaluation of specific sites identified during Phase I to determine National Register eligibility. Phase I covers broad areas with limited excavation; Phase II focuses intensively on individual sites with systematic test units.

Can a project proceed if a site is found eligible after Phase II?

Yes. A National Register eligibility finding does not halt a project. The lead federal agency must assess the effect and, if adverse, consult with the SHPO on mitigation. Options include avoiding the site through redesign, minimizing impacts through construction controls, or conducting Phase III data recovery excavation before the area is disturbed.

How much does Phase II archaeological evaluation cost?

Cost depends on site size, depth of deposits, laboratory needs, and the complexity of regional prehistory or history represented. Budgets typically range from tens of thousands to several hundred thousand dollars for large or deeply stratified sites. A detailed research design scoped to the specific site usually produces the most cost-efficient evaluation while satisfying regulatory requirements.

Plan Your Phase II Evaluation Early

A well-executed Phase II archaeological evaluation delivers more than regulatory compliance — it produces defensible data that protects project sponsors from downstream legal challenges and expensive redesigns. Understanding what the process involves, who must conduct it, and how findings shape project decisions allows agencies and developers to integrate cultural resources review into project planning rather than treating it as an obstacle.

TVAR's RPA-certified team brings more than 14 years of Phase I through Phase III experience across the Southeast. We combine rigorous field methodology with clear, agency-ready reporting to keep your project on schedule. Contact us to discuss your project's Phase II needs.
excerpt: Learn what Phase II archaeological evaluation involves, why it is required under Section 106, and how National Register eligibility is determined for your project site.
